question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / view reentrancy] Can an unprivileged callback receiver reenter EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified during the execution of Midnight.take (which calls isRatified as a view function at line 356), because isRatified is marked view but reads from Midnight's isAuthorized storage, and a malicious callback could modify isAuthorized state between the isRatified check and the position update, violating the invariant that callbacks must not corrupt partial state through reentrancy, causing scoped impact: authorization state observed by isRatified differs from state at position update time? Proof idea: deploy malicious callback that calls setIsAuthorized during onBuy, assert whether the authorization change affects the already-completed isRatified check.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz all signature fields] Can an unprivileged taker find any combination of (v, r, s, root, leafIndex, proof) that passes all checks in isRatified for an offer where the maker did not sign the root, by fuzzing the full ratifierData parameter space, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill without maker consent? Proof idea: stateful fuzz test with arbitrary (v, r, s) values, arbitrary root, arbitrary proof, assert isRatified always reverts with InvalidSignature or Unauthorized for unsigned roots.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / structHash type commitment] Can an unprivileged taker submit a proof of length N but a signature that was generated for a tree of height M (N != M), because the structHash uses offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length) (line 39) which commits to the tree height, causing the digest to differ from what the maker signed, violating the invariant that the EIP-712 type must match the actual tree structure, causing scoped impact: confirming that height mismatch is correctly rejected, or identifying if any height values produce colliding typehashes? Proof idea: sign root for height 2, submit ratifierData with proof.length=3 (padding with zero sibling), assert Unauthorized revert due to digest mismatch.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / cross-offer proof reuse] Can an unprivileged taker reuse the same ratifierData (sig, root, leafIndex, proof) for a different offer in the same Merkle tree by changing the offer passed to take, because isRatified verifies that the offer is a leaf in the tree (line 37) but the same proof could be valid for a different leaf at the same index, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the specific offer being filled, causing scoped impact: a taker fills offer B using ratifierData generated for offer A if both are at the same leaf index in different trees? Proof idea: build two trees with different offers at index 0, sign root1 for offer A, attempt take with offer B using ratifierData for offer A, assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: cancelRoot / event vs state mismatch] Can an unprivileged authorized operator call cancelRoot(maker, root) where root was never signed by the maker, setting isRootCanceled[maker][root] = true for a root that doesn't exist yet, preemptively blocking any future offer tree the maker might sign with that root value, violating the invariant that user funds must not be frozen outside valid protocol rules, causing scoped impact: an operator can preemptively cancel any future root the maker might sign, forcing the maker to generate roots that haven't been pre-canceled? Proof idea: operator calls cancelRoot for random bytes32 root, maker later signs that exact root, taker attempts take, assert RootCanceled revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz leafIndex boundary] Can an unprivileged taker submit a leafIndex that equals exactly 2^proof.length - 1 (maximum valid index) or 2^proof.length (one past maximum), testing the boundary condition of require(leafIndex >> proof.length == 0) in HashLib.isLeaf (line 58), violating the invariant that the Merkle proof must correctly bind the offer to its position, causing scoped impact: off-by-one in the range check could allow an out-of-range leafIndex to pass, enabling proof manipulation? Proof idea: fuzz leafIndex at 2^N-1, 2^N, 2^N+1 for proof.length=N, assert LeafIndexOutOfRange reverts exactly at leafIndex >= 2^N.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / authorization check order] Can an unprivileged taker observe that isRatified checks the Merkle proof (line 37) before checking root cancellation (line 38) before checking the signature (lines 39-44), meaning a canceled root still causes the Merkle proof to be verified before the cheaper cancellation check, violating the invariant that low-cost DoS must not block take, causing scoped impact: a griever can force expensive Merkle proof computation for canceled roots by submitting valid proofs with canceled roots? Proof idea: cancel a root, submit take with valid proof for canceled root, measure gas consumed in isRatified before RootCanceled revert, compare to gas if cancellation check were first.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz proof with zero siblings] Can an unprivileged taker submit a proof array containing all-zero bytes32 values, causing HashLib.isLeaf to compute a deterministic path through the tree using keccak256(currentHash, 0x00) at each level, violating the invariant that the Merkle proof must correspond to actual sibling hashes, causing scoped impact: if any offer hash combined with zero siblings produces a root that the maker coincidentally signed, the proof would be accepted? Proof idea: fuzz proof arrays with zero entries, compute resulting root, check if any maker has signed that root, assert isRatified rejects all-zero proofs for unsigned roots.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / MIDNIGHT immutable binding] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious contract at the address stored in MIDNIGHT (immutable, set at construction line 24), if the original Midnight contract is self-destructed or replaced via a proxy, causing isRatified to call isAuthorized on the malicious contract (line 44) which returns true for any query, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker, causing scoped impact: all offers become ratifiable without valid signatures if MIDNIGHT points to a compromised contract? Proof idea: deploy mock Midnight that returns true for all isAuthorized queries, deploy EcrecoverRatifier pointing to mock, assert isRatified accepts any signature.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz offer.maker vs signer] Can an unprivileged taker find an offer.maker address such that ecrecover returns that exact address for a crafted (v, r, s) tuple without knowing the private key, by fuzzing the signature components, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker, causing scoped impact: unauthorized offer fill for a maker whose address happens to be recoverable from a crafted signature? Proof idea: stateful fuzz test with random (v, r, s, offer.maker) combinations, assert ecrecover never returns offer.maker for unsigned digests across 10000 iterations.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: cancelRoot / authorized address can authorize others] Can an unprivileged authorized operator, who has been granted authorization by the maker in Midnight, call Midnight.setIsAuthorized to authorize a third party on behalf of the maker (because Midnight authorization is coarse-grained per Midnight.sol line 731-734), and then have that third party call cancelRoot for the maker, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: a chain of delegated authorizations allows a distant third party to cancel the maker's roots without direct maker consent? Proof idea: maker authorizes operator, operator calls setIsAuthorized(thirdParty, true, maker), thirdParty calls cancelRoot(maker, root), assert root is canceled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz tree height vs typehash] Can an unprivileged taker find two different proof lengths N and M such that offerTreeTypeHash(N) == offerTreeTypeHash(M), causing a signature for a height-N tree to be accepted as a height-M tree, violating the invariant that the EIP-712 type must uniquely identify the tree structure, causing scoped impact: a signature for a small tree could be replayed against a larger tree with a crafted proof? Proof idea: compare all 21 offerTreeTypeHash values (heights 0-20) for collisions, assert all are distinct.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz market collateral array ordering] Can an unprivileged taker submit an offer with collateralParams in a different order than what was signed, because hashMarket iterates collateralParams in order (HashLib.sol lines 91-93) and the resulting hash depends on ordering, violating the invariant that the maker's market terms must be immutable once signed, causing scoped impact: confirming that collateral ordering is enforced, or identifying if any permutation of collateralParams produces the same market hash? Proof idea: sign offer for market with collateralParams=[A, B], submit take with collateralParams=[B, A], assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz v value outside {27, 28}] Can an unprivileged taker submit a signature with v value outside {27, 28} (e.g., v=0, v=1, v=26, v=29), causing ecrecover to return address(0) (line 42), which then triggers the require(_signer != address(0)) check (line 43) to revert with InvalidSignature, confirming the guard is effective, or identifying if any non-standard v value produces a non-zero address that passes authorization? Proof idea: fuzz v over all uint8 values, assert ecrecover returns address(0) for v not in {27, 28} and the revert is InvalidSignature.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / state transition: authorize then take then revoke] Can an unprivileged maker create a race condition where an operator's signature is valid at the time of take submission but the maker revokes authorization in the same block (via multicall or front-running), causing the take to revert with Unauthorized mid-execution, violating the invariant that valid offers must be fillable until expiry, causing scoped impact: a maker can front-run a taker's take transaction by revoking operator authorization, effectively canceling the offer without calling cancelRoot? Proof idea: simulate front-run: taker submits take, maker submits setIsAuthorized(operator, false) with higher gas, assert take reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz ratifierData ABI decoding] Can an unprivileged taker submit malformed ratifierData that causes abi.decode to revert or produce unexpected values (line 35-36), because abi.decode with incorrect encoding can revert with a generic error rather than a specific ratifier error, violating the invariant that ratifier failures must produce clear error codes, causing scoped impact: a taker who accidentally provides malformed ratifierData gets an opaque revert instead of a helpful error, leading to user confusion and potential fund loss from failed transactions? Proof idea: fuzz ratifierData with random bytes, assert all reverts are either specific ratifier errors or standard ABI decode errors, never silent failures.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz proof with maximum height 20] Can an unprivileged taker submit a valid Merkle proof of height 20 (1048576 leaves) with a correctly signed root, testing whether the gas cost of isLeaf's 20-iteration loop combined with hashOffer's collateral array hashing causes the take transaction to exceed the block gas limit for markets with 128 collateral tokens, violating the invariant that valid takes must not be blocked by gas constraints, causing scoped impact: fund freeze for makers who use large Merkle trees in markets with many collateral tokens? Proof idea: deploy market with 128 collateral tokens, sign root for height-20 tree, attempt take with full proof, measure gas consumption.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: cancelRoot / event integrity] Can an unprivileged authorized operator call cancelRoot(maker, root) where msg.sender != maker, causing the CancelRoot event (line 30) to emit caller=operator, maker=maker, root=root, which could mislead off-chain monitoring systems that track cancellations by the maker address, violating the invariant that event data must accurately reflect the authorizing party, causing scoped impact: off-chain systems that filter CancelRoot events by maker address may miss operator-initiated cancellations, leading to stale offer distribution? Proof idea: operator calls cancelRoot for maker, assert CancelRoot event has caller=operator not caller=maker, verify off-chain indexer would correctly attribute the cancellation.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz same root different offers] Can an unprivileged taker find two distinct Offer structs A and B such that HashLib.hashOffer(A) == HashLib.hashOffer(B), creating a hash collision that allows ratifierData signed for offer A to be used to fill offer B, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the specific offer being filled, causing scoped impact: a taker could fill an offer with different terms (different tick, maxUnits, market) than what the maker signed? Proof idea: differential fuzz test comparing hashOffer outputs for all pairs of offers differing in one field, assert no two distinct offers produce the same hash.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / integration with Midnight.multicall] Can an unprivileged taker use Midnight.multicall to batch a take call that uses EcrecoverRatifier with a setIsAuthorized call that grants the taker authorization over the maker's position, because multicall uses delegatecall (Midnight.sol line 213) and processes calls sequentially, violating the invariant that multicall must not bypass per-action invariants, causing scoped impact: the taker gains maker authorization within the same multicall that fills the offer, enabling subsequent unauthorized position manipulation? Proof idea: construct multicall([take(offer), setIsAuthorized(taker, true, maker)]), assert setIsAuthorized fails because taker is not authorized for maker at that point.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz block.chainid at boundary] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that EcrecoverRatifier uses block.chainid (line 40) while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID (Midnight.sol line 185), by deploying on a chain where block.chainid == INITIAL_CHAIN_ID at construction but block.chainid changes later (e.g., testnet resets), causing pre-deployment signatures to become invalid while post-deployment signatures remain valid, violating the invariant that the domain separator must be stable for the lifetime of the contract, causing scoped impact: unpredictable signature validity windows for makers on chains with unstable chainIds? Proof idea: deploy EcrecoverRatifier, sign root, change vm.chainId, assert isRatified rejects pre-change signature, assert new signature with new chainId is accepted.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz proof sibling order] Can an unprivileged taker swap the order of siblings in a Merkle proof (providing the right sibling where the left is expected), causing HashLib.isLeaf to compute a different root because hashNode(A, B) != hashNode(B, A) (HashLib.sol line 67), violating the invariant that the Merkle proof must correctly reconstruct the signed root, causing scoped impact: confirming that sibling ordering is enforced by leafIndex bit extraction, or identifying if any symmetric tree structure allows order-independent proofs? Proof idea: build 2-leaf tree, swap proof sibling order, assert isLeaf returns false for swapped proof.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz authorized signer revocation timing] Can an unprivileged maker create a time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerability by revoking an operator's authorization between the moment a taker reads isAuthorized off-chain (to verify the offer is valid) and the moment the take transaction is mined, because isRatified checks isAuthorized at execution time (line 44), violating the invariant that valid offers must be fillable until expiry, causing scoped impact: takers who pre-verify offer validity off-chain may have their take transactions revert unexpectedly, losing gas and missing trading opportunities? Proof idea: simulate mempool delay, maker revokes authorization after taker signs take tx but before mining, assert take reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz proof.length == 1 with wrong sibling] Can an unprivileged taker submit a height-1 Merkle proof with an incorrect sibling hash but a correctly signed root that happens to equal hashNode(offerHash, wrongSibling), because the maker signed a root for a 2-leaf tree where the second leaf is the wrongSibling, violating the invariant that the Merkle proof must bind the offer to the specific tree the maker signed, causing scoped impact: confirming that proof verification is correct for height-1 trees, or identifying if the sibling hash is not sufficiently constrained? Proof idea: build 2-leaf tree with offers [A, B], sign root, submit proof for A with sibling=hashOffer(C) where C != B, assert InvalidProof revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: isRatified / fuzz EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH correctness] Can an unprivileged taker verify that EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH (IEcrecoverRatifier.sol line 14) equals keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') and that the domain separator computation (line 40) is correct, because an incorrect typehash constant would cause all signatures to be invalid, violating the invariant that the EIP-712 domain separator must be correctly computed, causing scoped impact: if the hardcoded typehash is wrong, all EcrecoverRatifier signatures are permanently invalid, freezing all maker offers? Proof idea: unit test asserting EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH == keccak256('EIP712Domain(uint256 chainId,address verifyingContract)') and OFFER_TYPEHASH, MARKET_TYPEHASH, COLLATERAL_PARAMS_TYPEHASH match their string definitions.
